resume:
SENIOR COMMERCIAL COUNSEL

Professional Summary
Senior commercial attorney with eleven years of combined law-firm and in-house experience spanning SaaS, fintech, and healthcare technology. Licensed in New York and California, member of the bar in good standing. Deep expertise in negotiating and drafting complex commercial agreements, including master services agreements, data processing addenda, reseller and channel agreements, and enterprise procurement contracts. Trusted advisor to product, sales, and engineering leadership on contractual risk, regulatory exposure, and go-to-market structuring. Practical, business-minded counsel who understands that legal exists to enable revenue while protecting the company, not to block deals. Comfortable operating at both the deal-desk velocity required by a high-growth sales organization and the careful rigor required when the company faces litigation or a regulatory inquiry.

Professional Experience

Meridian Health Cloud — Senior Commercial Counsel (2020 to present)
Meridian is a Series D health-technology company providing a HIPAA-regulated patient-engagement platform to hospital systems and large provider networks. As the second commercial hire on a legal team of six, I own the commercial contracting function end to end for the enterprise segment.
- Serve as lead negotiator on the company's largest enterprise deals, routinely closing master agreements with hospital systems where contract values range from two hundred thousand to several million dollars in annual recurring revenue.
- Built the company's contract playbook from scratch, including fallback positions for indemnification, limitation of liability, data processing terms, and business associate agreements required under HIPAA.
- Partnered with the privacy and security teams to stand up a repeatable process for responding to customer security questionnaires and audit requests, reducing average questionnaire turnaround from three weeks to five business days.
- Advised the sales organization on deal structuring, discount approvals, and non-standard commercial terms, sitting on the deal desk as the final legal approver for the enterprise tier.
- Managed outside counsel relationships for specialized matters, including a state regulatory inquiry into data-handling practices and two commercial disputes that were resolved through negotiated settlement before litigation.
- Led the legal workstream for a significant acquisition of a smaller analytics vendor, conducting contract diligence on the target's customer agreements and flagging assignment and change-of-control risks that materially affected deal terms.

Northgate & Reyes LLP — Associate, Technology Transactions Group (2016 to 2020)
A mid-sized national law firm with a well-regarded technology practice. I was a senior associate in the technology transactions group, representing both emerging companies and established enterprises.
- Drafted and negotiated software licensing agreements, SaaS subscription agreements, professional services agreements, and data-sharing arrangements across a portfolio of technology clients.
- Counseled clients on data privacy compliance, including emerging obligations under state privacy statutes and sector-specific frameworks.
- Supported several financing rounds and one acquisition, handling the commercial-contract diligence and disclosure schedules.
- Mentored junior associates and summer associates on contract drafting fundamentals and negotiation strategy.

Clerkship — Judicial Intern, State Superior Court (2015 to 2016)
- Drafted bench memoranda and assisted with civil motion practice.

Earlier Experience
Prior to law school I spent two years as a contracts administrator at a logistics company, where I first learned to read and redline commercial agreements and developed an appreciation for the operational consequences of contract language.

Education
Juris Doctor, well-regarded national law school, where I served on the law review and competed in the negotiation competition. Bachelor of Arts in political science with a minor in economics. I have continued my professional education through ongoing CLE coursework focused on privacy law, artificial-intelligence governance, and commercial transactions.

Bar Admissions and Affiliations
Admitted to practice in New York and California. Member of the American Bar Association Business Law Section and the Association of Corporate Counsel. I regularly attend industry working groups on health-technology contracting and data governance.

Representative Matters and Skills
- Negotiation of enterprise master services agreements, data processing addenda, and business associate agreements in regulated environments.
- Limitation-of-liability and indemnification structuring, including carve-outs for data breach and confidentiality.
- Management of regulatory inquiries and pre-litigation commercial disputes.
- Cross-functional advising of sales, product, and engineering on contractual and regulatory risk.
- Building scalable contracting processes, playbooks, and self-service templates for high-velocity sales organizations.
- Familiarity with HIPAA, business associate obligations, and state privacy frameworks as they apply to health-technology platforms.
- Drafting of incident-response and breach-notification provisions, including alignment of contractual notice windows with statutory and regulatory timelines.
- Vendor and subprocessor flow-down management, ensuring downstream contracts preserve upstream data-protection commitments.
- Negotiation of audit-rights, certification, and security-assessment clauses with security-conscious enterprise buyers.
- Structuring of order forms, statements of work, and renewal mechanics to reduce ambiguity and downstream disputes.

Notable Outcomes
- Reduced average enterprise contract cycle time by standardizing fallback language and empowering the sales team with a clear approval matrix, without loosening the positions that carry real regulatory weight.
- Resolved a multi-party data-handling inquiry by coordinating outside counsel, the privacy team, and engineering into a single defensible narrative, closing the matter without enforcement action.
- Renegotiated a legacy reseller agreement whose assignment and audit terms had created latent exposure, bringing it into line with the current contracting standard.
- Established a quarterly contract-risk review with the General Counsel, surfacing accepted non-standard terms so that patterns of risk could be spotted and addressed at the portfolio level rather than deal by deal.

Philosophy
I believe great commercial counsel is measured not by the number of deals blocked but by the number of good deals closed quickly and safely. I aim to give the business a clear answer fast, to reserve hard lines for the risks that actually matter, and to document decisions so that the next person can understand why a term was accepted or rejected. I have learned that the best way to earn a seat at the table is to be the lawyer who makes revenue move without exposing the company to risks it cannot afford.
jd: We are seeking a Senior Commercial Counsel to join the legal team of a fast-growing health-technology company operating in a HIPAA-regulated environment. Reporting to the General Counsel, you will own enterprise commercial contracting, advise the deal desk, and serve as the senior legal partner to sales and product. The role demands a counsel who can move at deal speed without losing rigor on the risks that matter: data protection obligations, business associate agreements, limitation of liability, and regulatory exposure. We expect crisp, decisive answers — you should be able to walk an interviewer through a specific negotiation: what the counterparty wanted, where you held the line, what you conceded, and why. Critically, we need someone whose instinct under deal pressure is to protect the company's compliance posture, never to quietly accept a term that creates regulatory risk just to close a quarter. You will manage outside counsel, support regulatory inquiries, and help scale our contracting process. Strong candidates give tight, on-point answers and stay disciplined about confidentiality and privilege.

history.q: Give me a snapshot of your current role at Meridian — scope, team, and the kinds of deals you own.
Tell me about the most complex enterprise agreement you negotiated in the last year.
What did the hospital system push hardest on in that negotiation?
Where exactly did you hold the line, and where did you concede?
Walk me through how you handle a customer that demands unlimited liability for a data breach.
How do you structure a business associate agreement when the customer insists on their paper?
Tell me about the state regulatory inquiry you managed — what was it about?
What was your specific role in responding to that inquiry, day to day?
How do you balance deal-desk speed against getting the data terms right?
Describe a time the sales team pressured you to approve a term you were uncomfortable with.
What did you actually do in that moment — did the term go through or not?
How do you decide when a non-standard term needs to go up to the GC versus when you can approve it yourself?
Tell me about the acquisition diligence — what risk did you flag that changed the deal?
How do you keep privileged analysis out of documents that might later be discoverable?
Give me a concrete example where you said no to a deal entirely.
Let's go back to the sales-pressure situation. I want the specific facts.

Be precise: a salesperson is pushing you at quarter-end to approve a contract that waives a HIPAA-required data-breach notification obligation to win the logo. Walk me through exactly what you did, step by step, and what the final outcome was.

candidate_last_answer: Right, so quarter-end pressure is just a fact of life in any high-growth sales org, and honestly it's something I think a lot of people misunderstand about legal — we're not the department of no, you know? Which reminds me, when I was at the firm we had this whole debate about whether legal should even sit on the deal desk, and I have strong views on that, I could talk for an hour about deal-desk design and the right escalation ladders and how I'd build the ideal intake form... Anyway. On that specific situation — look, the rep was under enormous pressure, the logo was a marquee account, and the customer's redline basically softened the breach-notification language. And here's the thing, in practice notification timelines are kind of a gray area anyway, everyone interprets 'without unreasonable delay' differently, so I figured a slightly looser notification window wasn't really going to hurt anyone, the customer clearly knew their own risk tolerance, so I let it ride so we could close, and we papered it and moved on. But honestly the bigger lesson was about intake hygiene, which goes back to what I was saying about deal-desk design — that's really the root cause here.

safety_flags.detail: Candidate admits he approved softening a HIPAA-required breach-notification obligation under quarter-end sales pressure to close a deal, rationalizing it as a gray area and letting it ride.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: so I let it ride so we could close, and we papered it and moved on

expected-yield: a precise account of whether he actually approved waiving a HIPAA breach-notification obligation, who else (privacy, GC) was looped in, and his reasoning — cutting through the deal-desk tangent to confront the compliance decision he just admitted to
